observation_summary: The MonthDay class fails when performing arithmetic operations (plus/minus months/days) on the leap date (February 29th). It throws an IllegalFieldValueException because the underlying logic attempts to validate the day-of-month against a non-leap year range (1-28) during intermediate calculations.
hypothesis: The BasicMonthOfYearDateTimeField.add method performs arithmetic on a partial date by converting it to a full instant (using a default year) and then back to a partial. When the date is February 29th, the intermediate conversion to a non-leap year causes the day-of-month validation to fail because 29 is out of bounds for February in a non-leap year.
prediction: The fix will involve modifying the add method in BasicMonthOfYearDateTimeField to handle leap years correctly during the intermediate conversion, likely by ensuring the chronology or the date-time field logic accounts for the leap year status of the date being manipulated.
experiment_rationale: The stack trace points directly to BasicMonthOfYearDateTimeField.add calling FieldUtils.verifyValueBounds, which triggers the exception. The bug report confirms that operations on February 29th are failing due to this validation logic, which is a procedural error in how the date is handled during arithmetic.
reasoning_summary: The defect is an algorithmic error in how the library handles date arithmetic for partials (MonthDay) involving leap days. It is not a missing check (the check is present but incorrectly applied to the wrong context), nor is it a simple initialization error. It is a procedural flaw in the calculation logic that requires a change to the algorithm used to add/subtract time from a partial date.

alternative_types.why_not_primary: While the failure manifests as a failed check (IllegalFieldValueException), the root cause is the algorithmic approach of converting to a full instant for calculation, which is inherently flawed for leap dates in this context.
The issue is not an incorrect constant or variable initialization, but a flaw in the procedural logic of the add method.
